# Landscaping & Lawn Care Industry Snapshot — TheDealSheet

**Author:** TheDealSheet Editorial
**Source:** [thedealsheet.co](https://thedealsheet.co/industries/landscaping)
**Saved:** 2026-04-20

## Why I Saved This

Landscaping was REJECTED earlier in G&B niche review. Captured TheDealSheet PE-POV data to (a) validate the rejection against fresh industry benchmarks and (b) stress-test whether a luxury sub-niche (estate grounds / HNW residential / country clubs / luxury hospitality) warrants a carve-out reactivation. Part of the 2026-04-20 TDS PE-POV cross-reference — see [[traces/agents/2026-04-20-tds-pe-pov-xref]].

## Content

### Market Size & Growth
- U.S. market: $188.8B (2025)
- CAGR: 5.7% (2025-2030), sustained through 2033
- Total operators: 726,565 businesses (extremely fragmented)

### Revenue Mix
- Maintenance Services: 91.5% of revenue (recurring backbone)
  - Residential: 61% of maintenance
  - Commercial & Institutional: 39% of maintenance
- Design-Build & Hardscaping: 8.5% (project-based, non-recurring)

### Fragmentation
- Top 50 market share: 20% (vs ~30% in commercial cleaning — MORE fragmented)
- PE-backed platforms: 10-15% sponsor-owned (minority)

### Margins (G&B buy-box: $2-10M revenue)
| Metric | Industry | Top quartile |
|---|---|---|
| Gross margin | 40-50% | 55%+ |
| EBITDA margin | **10-15%** | 18-22% |
| Customer retention | 75-85% | 90%+ |
| Route density (stops/day) | 8-12 | 15+ |
| Revenue per truck | $150K-$200K | $250K+ |

**Service-level gross margins:**
- Lawn Mowing: 35-45%
- Fertilization/Weed Control: 40-50%
- Irrigation Maintenance: 30-40%
- Hardscape Installation: 15-25% (project, not recurring)
- Design-Build: 20-30% (project)
- Commercial Grounds: 25-35% (recurring)

Median SDE: $215K / Median sale price: $645K.

### Valuation Multiples
| Band | Multiple | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| $250K-$500K | 1.7-2.2x | SDE |
| $500K-$1.5M | 2.5-3.0x | SDE |
| $1.5M-$5M | 2.8-3.5x | SDE |
| $5M-$10M+ | 3.2-4.0x | SDE |

Platform exits trade 11-14x EBITDA (arbitrage spread: 3-4x single-asset vs 11-14x platform). Premium band 4.0-6.0x requires: >75% recurring, commercial ESG contracts, geographic density, tech stack, certified crews, multi-service.

### Recurring Revenue
- New bookings via subscription: 75%+
- Retention: 75-85% industry / 90%+ top quartile
- >60% recurring commands premium multiple
- >75% recurring = platform-scale threshold

### PE Activity & Named Platforms
M&A pace: 2022 ~100 → 2023 ~100 → 2024 138 (+32% YoY) → 2025 ~108 (-22% YoY PE add-on softness). 2025 PE participation: 76% of deals.

Named platforms:
- American Landscaping Partners (Shoreline Equity Partners) — 7 add-ons since Aug 2023
- Landscape Workshop (Ares Management) — 12+ add-ons, Southeast/mid-Atlantic commercial
- Perennial Services Group (Brentwood Associates + Tenex Capital) — 25+ add-ons, multi-brand (lawn/pest/irrigation/tree); recapped Jan 2026
- ExperiGreen Lawn Care (Wind Point Partners) — 40K → 140K+ customers, Midwest residential
- SavATree — 33+ tree/arboriculture acquisitions
- U.S. Lawns (Riverside Company) — 250+ franchise territories

### Operational Challenges (severe)
- 171,600 open positions; 51-80% of operators cite staffing shortage
- Turnover: 31% (better than commercial cleaning's 75-200%, but still painful)
- Average wage $39K; wage growth 5.8%/yr; projected 20% wage inflation through 2029
- H-2B visa 2025: 97K requested / <65K approved — structural labor gap
- Seasonal layoffs: 49% of businesses do them; >40% workforce reductions in Northern regions
- Northern climate revenue variance: 50%+
- Input costs 39.5% above Feb 2020 (materials) — structural
- Annual compliance: $3K-$8K/yr

### Premium Drivers (what earns 4.0-6.0x)
1. Recurring maintenance >75% of revenue
2. Commercial multi-year ESG contracts (15-20% price premium)
3. Geographic route density
4. CRM / FSM / GPS fleet tech stack
5. Certified crew (irrigation, pesticide, hardscape)
6. Multi-service portfolio

### Disqualifiers (drag to 2.0-2.5x)
- Construction/hardscape-heavy mix
- >40% seasonal workforce cut
- Owner-dependent sales/crew mgmt
- Single customer >20% revenue
- Aging equipment / capex-due
- No compliance/licensing infrastructure

### Luxury Sub-Niche Signals
- 73% of homeowners cite outdoor kitchens as top upgrade (Gen Z/Millennial 47-65% more garden spend)
- Sun Belt migration (FL/TX/AZ) — year-round demand, no seasonality discount
- Corporate ESG: 15-20% premium for native plants / water-efficient systems
- Smart irrigation: $5.8B by 2033 (12% CAGR)
- Battery equipment: $5B by 2035 (6.8% CAGR)
- Top 10 metros: Miami, Phoenix, Austin, Charlotte, Tampa, DFW, Atlanta, Nashville, Raleigh, Houston

NOTE: TheDealSheet does NOT call out an explicit "estate grounds / HNW residential" sub-niche with benchmark margins. Arboriculture (SavATree) is the closest specialty play with higher-touch economics, but still faces core landscaping labor dynamics.

### Regulatory (2026)
- CA Landscape License Exemption threshold increase (Q1 2025)
- EPA Revised Pesticide Applicator Certification (Q3 2025)
- FL Fertilizer Application Restrictions Expansion (Q2 2025)
- NC Pesticide Certification Training Standards (Q2 2026)

### IRR Scenarios (TDS modeled)
- SBA buyer ($750K): 28-35% IRR, $1.2M exit (4.0x SDE)
- PE add-on ($2M): 35-45% IRR, $4.5M exit (6.0x EBITDA)
- Strategic buyer ($5M): 40-55% IRR, $15M exit (8.0x EBITDA)

## Key Patterns

- Pattern 1: **EBITDA margins (10-15%) are structurally below G&B's 25%+ premium-services floor.** Top quartile (18-22%) still misses the floor. Commercial cleaning at 12-18% industry / 20%+ top was already a stretch; landscaping is worse on margin despite better retention.
- Pattern 2: **Revenue mix tilts residential (61% of maintenance).** Residential landscaping is the commoditized, labor-commodity segment where G&B's luxury-services thesis has no moat. Commercial grounds (39% of maintenance) is the thesis-shaped slice but competes head-on with Landscape Workshop (Ares) and American Landscaping Partners (Shoreline) platforms already 7-12+ deals deep.
- Pattern 3: **Seasonality is structural, not operational.** 49% of businesses do seasonal layoffs; Northern revenue variance 50%+. Unlike commercial cleaning (year-round essential), landscaping can't be fixed via tech or positioning — only by geographic constraint to Sun Belt. This is a hard negative against Kay's NY-centric relationship base.
- Pattern 4: **Labor crisis is more structural than cleaning.** H-2B visa gap (97K requested / <65K approved) + 20% projected wage inflation + 31% turnover means labor cost is a permanent EBITDA headwind, not a fixable issue. Immigration policy volatility (Dignity Act) adds execution risk.
- Pattern 5: **PE has already built the platforms G&B would need to out-compete.** 76% of 2025 deals are PE; 10-15% of supply is sponsor-owned. American Landscaping Partners, Landscape Workshop, Perennial Services, ExperiGreen, SavATree, U.S. Lawns = 6 named consolidators, all better-capitalized than G&B. Multi-service / cross-sell thesis (lawn + pest + irrigation + tree) is already executed by Perennial Services Group.
- Pattern 6: **No explicit luxury sub-niche in TDS data.** Outdoor kitchens / HNW spending growth show demand, but neither is mapped to a scaled acquirable operator archetype. Estate grounds / country club grounds / luxury hospitality grounds are plausible but un-validated here — would require standalone niche-intelligence research, not a simple sub-niche carve.
- Pattern 7: **Arbitrage spread is real but not unique.** 3-4x single-asset → 11-14x platform is attractive math, but exists in commercial cleaning, pest, and most services niches. Not a landscaping-specific edge.

## How I Might Use This

- **Confirm the rejection.** The 10-15% EBITDA floor + residential-heavy mix + structural seasonality + PE saturation is a cleaner "no" than commercial cleaning's. TheDealSheet data CONFIRMS G&B's original rejection rather than challenging it.
- **If reconsidering, only as a geographically-bounded luxury sub-niche:** Sun Belt estate grounds maintenance for HNW residential ($5M+ homes) or ultra-luxury hospitality (resort grounds, country clubs). This would require its own niche-intelligence pass — TDS data is insufficient to validate sub-niche economics.
- **Competitive screen for any future DealsX deals:** exclude targets already owned by the 6 named PE platforms.
- **Cross-reference for storage thesis:** landscaping's failure modes (labor commodity + seasonality + PE saturation) are the inverse of what makes Storage for HV Assets work (low labor + year-round + specialty moat). Useful contrast for investor conversations about why G&B picks the niches it does.
- **Do NOT reactivate landscaping on TDS evidence alone.** A reactivation trigger should be (a) a Kay relationship-sourced luxury grounds operator surfacing organically, or (b) a Saltoun / Guillermo-level advisor pointing to a specific sub-segment. Not TDS optimism.
